commentBody:
Quick one today, to reach my 1600 day streak...I'll drink to that!
Cheers!
Well, today, it’s probably about tennis. (22A)

Another very nice Monday puzzle. When the clues aren't hard, and the theme gives you a third check on so many letters, it's hard to go wrong. It was fun. 
Thanks, Brad.
For some reason, even though I finished the puzzle, it's still running the clock. Any idea what to do about that?

I left the theme entries alone until I could [tea]se out the rules and [match a]nswers to said rule; but as this was a Tuesday puzzle, the theme was an "admire after the fact" more than "required for solving / understanding the answers". 

Fun, with good Tuesday clues. And not only is ENNEAD familiar to Spelling [Bee r]espondents (really just players but I couldn't resist), I just realized that's why enneagram is called that. 

Cheers.

MAKEMINEADOUBLE (Drink request appropriate for this puzzle?), also 15 letters, was my immediate guess for the revealer—it would have been fun to find a three drink example for this one so someone could go past the minimum!

I thought this was fairly chewy for a Tuesday—not hard per se but had a lot of sparkling entries (even though no ASTI included), and I jumped around a bit more than my average Tuesday.

No one told me geography was going to be a heavy requirement today.

Thanks Brad—my thirst for a fun Tuesday was sated.
I don’t love the clue for  “PENTATONIC SCALE”. I can see how it “progresses” but the term progression in music usually refers to a sequence of chords changes like a 2-5-1.
"Buffalo Bill" (aka Jaime Gumb) is the villain in The Silence of the Lambs, not Hannibal Lecter.

I didn't fall for the overeducated=overthinkinh trap today.

42A, Heads of Hogwarts?

Hogwarts has four houses, each with their head. McGonagall (Gryffindor), Snape (Slytherin), Flitwick (Ravenclaw), and Pomphrey (Hufflepuff). But then there's that pesky question mark. So what else can a head mean? A bathroom (which actually forms a major plot point in three of the seven books). And what would a head be called in Britain. A LOO, of course
Fun but felt hard for a Tuesday!

Most of the puzzle was pretty easy, especially figuring out the theme so early.  But there were a handful of clues that took me from solving in under ten minutes to nearly fifteen.

ENNEADS crossed with SERE was pure Natick for me, and then BOHO.
Hmmm.  Radar was an Iowan, but I'm pretty sure Hawkeye was from Maine.

@Nikita

1939 produced a great many fine movies.  From Wikipedia:
The ten films nominated for Best Picture at the 12th Academy Awards—Dark Victory, Gone with the Wind, Goodbye, Mr. Chips, Love Affair, Mr. Smith Goes to Washington, Ninotchka, Of Mice and Men, Stagecoach, The Wizard of Oz, and Wuthering Heights—range in genre and are considered classics.

I suspect that many would say "the defining film of 1939" was Gone With The Wind considering the state of the Union at the time.

I'm on fire.

Two in a row!  A streak!

But I've never heard of a "gut course".

Just the other day, having dinner and wine with friends, we toasted quite a few times whilst covering a range of whatever "cheers" we could think of: Salud! Prost! Saúde! Cin-Cin! Skål! L'Chaim! If you're wondering who does that, with an eye roll, I'm pleased to tell you: Those who have already passed the TWO DRINK MINIMUM, and resort to the silliest things to have fun. But for those who'll be enjoying a *grown-up* dinner with wine tonight, with a TWO DRINK MaxiMUM, here is Mama CASS to set the mood: https://youtu.be/v8I5vDewcZo?si=4sG_-Xzc6YyaDa3O For me this was bit of a tough Tuesday puzzle, which usually means that the fill is varied and rich. Not only did that make it terrific, it also allowed me to be smugly proud to have thought of NIGER with no crosses. (Especially after not being able to think of the simplest DIET for [What you eat], only "food" or "junk," and having CAPE Cod before CAPE ANN, and SOLO act before SOLOIST, just to name a few foibles.) Thank you, Mr. Wiegmann. I hope we'll see you back soon with a Saturday so we can toast to you hitting the cycle. Here's rooting for you!

For 'what you eat,' I had 'dirt' filled in for a while. 

I was thinking a phrasing like 'what to eat if accepting blame' would have been nicer 😅
SERE / ENNEADS is a tough crossing for a Tuesday.
Now I really want S’mores…
cool theme, but too hard for a tuesday. ive never heard of TCBY, SERE, or another name for a sailor being TAR? maybe i'm too young but some of these answers mean absolutely nothing to me
I found this to be a breezy Tuesday (fun theme!), but then "the SERE, the yellow leaf" is never too far from my mind, as I age.

For those trying for recovery or a ONEDRINKMINIMUM:

1A [Bindle]
HO(BOBA)G

2A {Famous writer about Samoa]
MARGARET(MEAD)

2A [Not a knuckle sandwich]
HAWAIIAN(PUNCH)
Whew. That was TRICKY for a Tuesday. I struggled with the NW corner but somehow still finished faster than average with no lookups. Clever one and figuring out the theme really helped.

Head is a loo only on a boat. 

Hogwarts isn’t a boat. 

The puzzle was good. A bit tough for a Tuesday.
He abides

Sometimes constructors seem jaded and cook up puzzles that are less crosswords than conundrums.  When I first looked at the empty grid I thought we might be in for one of those, but this one was just fine.  Words were the key and I'll drink to that.
I'm going to need you to explain that LOOS clueing to me again slowly. What the?

impecCABle cheWINEss.
That’s how I would describe this puzzle, and it has nothing at all to do with my own two drink minimum. I swear.

I was ultimately done in by the unknown cross of S_RE and ENN_ADS. I tried every vowel, but none of them worked. Turns out, I had also misplaced a letter elsewhere. D’oh!

I might need a little mocha… or maybe just a little mo’ chablis.

Really tough

Don't love CHAI as part of the theme, since it's the lone non-alcoholic drink

(TEA could be shorthand for "Long Island Ice Tea")

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: That was a Tuesday hard-hat tuna hoagie after a delicate Monday Mushroom Palmiers appetizer.

Great theme, well executed! Make mine a double! 

However:

I take umbrage at CCCP because those are Cyrillic letters, and the English transliteration would be SSSR. 

I also harrumph somewhat at LOOS because “heads” is not so much British English as nautical language. The “heads” are the loos on a ship, just as the kitchen is the “galley”. What do American sailors call the ablutions on USS Gerald R. Ford?

I stoutly resist the TCBY/TBS cross as suitable Tuesday fodder! 

I confess with some embarrassment that i got stuck on Denali because i thought it was Diwali, and the best abbreviation i could think of was MTH. 

However, i smugly appreciate SERE, because i know my Macbeth, who laments:

“My way of life
Is fall'n into the sere, the yellow leaf”
Just had to look up the gender agreement for amie. Exceptions to French language rules creating additional complexity for this overthinker…

Tough one for a Tuesday by my lights. About three minutes above my average.

There were a couple of obscure ones, but what really held me up was getting stuck on SOLOacT, which dislodged KID.

Good start to the day.
Enjoyed the theme, however now I want a drink and its too early....  Oh well.

Not a Tuesday as far as I'm concerned. Americans may have found it easier as the TCBS and TBY and CAPEANN with GINNIE were unknowns to me. (although the latter was guessable if I had had all the other letters. which I didn't as "TOWAWAY" and "COWPOSE" were equally obscure to me.

Any puzzle with ENNEADS crossing PENTATONIC SCALE is OK fine in my book! Although, like several other musical overthinkers, [Standard musical progression] implies a harmonic chord progression, not a scale--perhaps "sequence" would have been a better clue. Here's what Leonard Bernstein had to say about the pentatonic scale--"The universality of this scale is so well known that I'm sure you could give me examples of it, from all corners of the earth, as from Scotland, or from China, or from Africa, and from American Indian cultures, from East Indian cultures, from Central and South America, Australia, Finland ... now, that is a true musico-linguistic universal." Here's a couple of favorites which pop immediately to mind: From Java, to Paris, then on to New York: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yc5nnT_S984&list=RDYc5nnT_S984&start_radio=1 From Celtic British Isles, to Appalachia: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WuA3V5zcqFI&list=RDWuA3V5zcqFI&start_radio=1

Took a bit, but got there!
Never heard of PENTATONIC SCALE.  But once the two drink miminimum theme became clear I was able to work backwards, ya know?

Do we still need to satisfy the emus?

B

I thought this one was quite hard for a Tuesday but also fun and satisfying. Question for the constructors out there - I was surprised that “The” showed up in both the clue and the answer for THEDUDE - is that a gray area? Or always fine to repeat a common word if necessary?

Anyone else see something geographically wrong with 46 across? It’s incorrect. Am I missing something? 

I haven’t finished yet, so I don’t wanna read all the comments. More soon!
Maybe the editors and columnist should have realized after writing up fourteen “tricky clues” that this was ill-suited for a Tuesday. Just a thought.
I knew it was wrong, but I badly wanted 29A to be tuna, as in "You can tune a piano but you can't tuna fish".
too much trivia! Never have I had to look up so many answers because I was stuck both ways. If you need that much trivia to fill your grid...

The theme struck me as rather uninteresting, but the fill was lively enough and seemed a bit meaty for a Tuesday.

Would have been better had there been a thematic entry with three embedded drinks. Can anyone find one that would not exceed the width of a weekday grid? Seems unlikely that such a thing exists.

Bit on the slow side for a Tuesday for me, but just had a good time working things out from the crosses. And... was almost done before I caught on to the theme trick. Nice 'aha' moment. And, of course a puzzle find. I'm sure I did this one but of course had completely forgotten. Anyway... a Wednesday from June 26, 2024 by Rebecca Goldstein. First three theme clues and answers: "*Jesus." SEAOFGALILEE "*Dorothy Gale." YELLOWBRICKROAD "*Neil Armstrong." TRANQUILITYBASE And the 'reveal.' "Memorable ad-lib in "Midnight Cowboy"... or what the starred clues would say about their answers." IMWALKINHERE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=6/26/2024&g=24&d=A ....

Wow, I was astounded to read all the comments and see that almost everyone thought this puzzle was hard. First, I kept filling in answer after answer that was pure crosswordese. ACNE, SMORE, SERE, GESTE, ENNEAD, and many others that I learned from crosswords. Maybe it's because I've been doing them since before many here were born. But because of that, it made it somewhat boring. Once the first two themers were deduced, the rest were easy to see, at least for me.

Second, unlike yesterday's, the theme answers were outlined, adding to the lack of difficulty. That would have been more fitting for the previous day's puzzle.

Yes, there were a couple of answers that were a little thorny, e.g. CHAIR UMPIRE, which I've never seen before, and PENTATONIC SCALE, which I have heard of but didn't easily come to mind.

I did like the revealer a lot, but overall, the puzzle was just meh. As Deb would say, YMMV. And apparently, mine did.

SMORES were never worth the mess; what about hand pies? Baked apples?  Who needs desserts?

Oh, the puzzle.  I seem to recall this constructor's name.
Say, Brad:  What if there were no boxes, and we had to do the word-search after the solve?  Two for one!  Fun!

The heat, the humidity, and the mosquitos are back here in MizSippy, and I got a dose of all three this morning, filling our vacationing neighbors' bird feeders.  Mrs. Mallard with 6 half-grown ducklings was willing to come close, so I stayed to fend off the pushy drakes who wanted the largess for themselves.  My reward was an impressive splotch where a skeeter apparently camped out; *I* was dinner and dessert.

Don’t usually mind puzzles that are more of a challenge, but I think there were too many proper nouns and names in this one. CASS, IOWAN, CAINE, TCBY, CAPE ANN, NIGER, FIDO, CCCP, AER, SUE, UCLA, FEY, ARI, TBS, OWEN, THE DUDE, LECTER, EDYS, GINNIE. While I did know most of these (and enjoyed quite a few), there were a couple that I’d never heard of (I was stuck on CASS, CCCP, TBS and TCBY in the end), and it’s always less satisfying when you have to guess answers to get the star.
So there was this one bar in Austin that had a TWO DRINK MaxIMUM on their margaritas. Well, I couldn't let that challenge go unanswered. Phew, they weren't kidding.

I call no fair on the crossing of 36A and 33D for us overseas solvers! Running the alphabet added a few minutes on my solve time.
Other than that, a fun grid. The boxes made the theme standout, happy to see GIN and TEA in there.
I'll be honest, this wasn't a good fit for a Tuesday puzzle. Would have been perfect for Thursday.

A very strong Tuesday puzzle, I think.  The revealer is absolutely inspired -- I did fill it in based on just TW-D and nothing else.  And finding those embedded drinks in such interesting and diverse answers as PENTATONIC SCALE, CHAIR UMPIRE and TWIN ENGINES is a real coup.  TEAM SPORT is somewhat less exciting, but it rounds out the symmetry.  Do you know how hard it is to come up with this sort of theme and make it symmetrical too?  A really good job!

I had a problem today that I'm sure was unique to me -- one that could not have happened to someone who solves online or who has better vision than I do (a low bar).  I had the "L" from LECTER filled in on the 42 square -- making the 42 look like a 47.  And so I had LO-S for the (I thought) clue: "Matching pair on many jets."  What on earth could that be??  When LOOS eventually came in -- there was no avoiding it -- I cried "Foul!"   Unless it's British Airways, why would you call them LOOS?  And do they actually "match"?

But I had the wrong clue for LOOS.  There's a Hogwarts one I didn't see.  And TWIN ENGINES is the answer to the matching jets pair.  Whew!

This, I'm sure, must have caused me extra solving time for no good reason.  Isn't it lucky that I pay absolutely no attention to such a thing as solving time?
I agree with some of the comments. This puzzle theme was easy, but the cluing was awkward, making it more like a Wednesday or Thursday puzzle. The mismatch made it a little annoying to solve.

(Couldn't resist one more . . .)

As a catechized and confirmed Neoplatonist (in the Church of St. Hypatia, Virgin Martyr, no less), it gave me a shock to see--Oh Heavenly Nous!--our holy writ, The Enneads, so casually mentioned!
NYT, do better!

My early post was emu-ed; tried it again a couple of hours later to the same result.  We’ll see if it pops up later.  Executive summary: an impressive build, a fun ride, a lovely theme echo in RYES, and lovely to see BASS close to ALE.

SW corner was killer with all the nouns. And I can never remember the ice cream brand because we don’t have it here. Finally got it but not so fun. And I don’t drink. But I got there. Not inappropriate for a Tuesday just inappropriate for me.

I was amused to see BASS in the vicinity of ALE. 

I didn't find this a Toughened Up Tuesday,  but generally consider them compensation for easier Thursday and Friday cluing.
Thought it was a little difficult for a Tuesday-also I have never heard of a gut course.

Oof, as a Brit the frozen dessert and Atlanta network cross was brutal. Plus TOWAWAY zone isn't something I'm familiiar with, although guessable. Plus plus I don't instinctively see Harry Potter as "British" in a clue, so the possible mitigation there didn't come through for me.
My absolute nightmare is the clue "Sch. whose campus is just off Interstate 405" ... Almost as impenetrable as the "Dir. from Topeka to Chattanooga" kind!
I'm really trying to learn American stuff, so I'm not complaining, just whinging a bit :)
Got there in the end, and the theme was very nicely done. Cheers Brad!
Enneads!? Why not the ancient Egyptian word for groups of six or seven too? Lame, dude!

Random thoughts:
•	I wonder why they used rectangles to highlight the drinks rather than circles.
•	Could have left the rectangles out altogether, actually, and made this a Wednesday.
•	I wish NIGER could have been TIGER.  Then, with the previous answer, we would have had “EASY, TIGER!”
•	Very nice touch by Brad – none of the drinks are stand-alone, as, say, the last four letters of “choCOLAte MILK” are.  This made the theme answers far more interesting.
•	As did their pop.  Four are NYT debuts.
•	No only did Brad find five theme answers that hid two drinks, but they also fit symmetry.  Wow!
•	Lovely theme echo in RYES, and lovely to see BASS close to ALE.

My brain loved guessing at the revealer, on top of all this other fun.  A spry springboard to the day – thanks, Brad!

(second try)

Random thoughts:
• I wonder why they used rectangles to highlight the drinks rather than circles.
• Could have left the rectangles out altogether, actually, and made this a Wednesday.
• I wish NIGER could have been TIGER. Then, with the previous answer, we would have had “EASY, TIGER!”
• Very nice touch by Brad – none of the drinks are stand-alone, as, say, the last four letters of “choCOLAte MILK” are. This made the theme answers far more interesting.
• As did their pop. Four are NYT debuts.
• No only did Brad find five theme answers that hid two drinks, but they also fit symmetry. Wow!
• Lovely theme echo in RYES, and lovely to see BASS close to ALE.

My brain loved guessing at the revealer, on top of all this other fun. A spry springboard to the day – thanks, Brad!
Just goes to show that with some creativity you can make a crossword out of any phrase.  TWO DRINK MINIMUM…who would have thought!

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ slowly slowly would you king
the whale the whale
the sea the sea
and you who sound
the horses free
d/ slowly slowly canopy
and the star slowly falling
and maybe it’s a bird
and all the lambs
a/ seep away
Um, sailors are called “tars” because they applied tar to the ships.

Saturday: “Victor over Washington on 11/12/1955 in "Back to the Future, Pt. II" — and in real life”

Monday: “West Coast sch. that joined the Big Ten in 2024”

Tuesday “Sch. whose campus is just off Interstate 405”

We sure are learning a lot about UCLA.

I've was familiar with several USA clues from this forum, which helped today. We've had discussion about TCBY (last week?), and EDDYS, and I've learnt about SMORE's, ENNEAD, gorp, CPA's, TOW AWAY.

I was sure the tumbling blocks must be jengas. SOLOIST is great!
I need a bit of help (eg GINNIE) but despite the names, I found this very enjoyable. 

SOUPS ON has no connection to soupçon, which is a pity.
I was pretty lucky to live near a TCBY (yes, they have those on Jupiter), and was able to get that one. What did not escape my notice is having two Harry Potter clues in two days...

What a fun Tuesday puzzle! For me it was a bit crunchy but not as frustrating as the two weekend puzzles were. There were some clever misdirections that led me astray and a few times I thought I was done and would hear the happy music, but then had to go looking for my mistake. Lately I've been rereading all the Harry Potter books so I was delighted to see the Hogwarts clue. But that was my final mistake in need of correcting--as I had also put in hECTOR in place of LECTER--so for a long time I had hOOd in place of LOOS.
Thanks for the fun and challenge, Brad Wiegmann. And thank you also Nikita Richardson, for a delightful column.
Right now I'm watching a beautiful plump California quail in the back yard looking for bugs and the nuts and seeds I put out for all the birds everyday. And I've learned to make peace with all the squirrels, foxes, and deer who sometimes share in the bounty. All God's Critters Got a Place in the Choir ;-)

ENNEADS x SERE
LECTER x CAPEANN x EDYS x GINNIE x NIGER x GESTE ....?!?!

Although I solved a few minutes faster than my norm I feel this was a sloppy puzzle. 

I liked the theme idea, but feel it strayed a little. Comedy clubs mostly serve alcohol and I think the majority of people that think of the phrase to drink minimum, think alcohol. Throwing in CHAI and TEA felt cheap.

Unfortunately, that cheap THEME seemed to flow in many other parts of the puzzle. 

Rating:
3/10

As a CHAIR UMPIRE I officiated at Wimbledon from 1961-1986. I officiated the now-infamous McEnroe/Gullikson match in which McEnroe removed his pants and did “the worm” for thirty-six minutes on the grass court.  Tennis has changed considerably since those days, some would argue for the better.

Ok I know I can’t be the only one who is irked by MON AMIE. 

“Amie” is feminine, so it should be MA AMIE. “MA” = feminine, “MON” = masculine. MON AMI would be correct if you’re using  “MON”.

Buffalo Bill was the villain in Silence of the Lambs, not Dr. Lecter. He was in prison, for his own heinous crimes, until the end when he "had an old friend for dinner". 
The puzzle was challenge enough, for a Tuesday, without inaccurate clues.

Roughly a month ago, doing the crossword puzzle in the games app started to brutally drain my phone’s battery. It doesn’t happen with any of the other games, just the crossword. After a few minutes of solving, I’ll notice my phone starting to get really hot, and then I’ll look at the battery and see that it’s losing charge as if it were a ten year old phone rather than a brand new one. Can anyone here relate to this? Is the NYT working on fixing this issue?
A bit slyer than the average Tuesday. I like that.

I’ve been playing for about a year now, thanks to my wonderful American in-laws who generously gift me a yearly NYT subscription. I thought I finally had the main American terms down pat, only to be humbled by today’s puzzle. I’ve never seen a TCYB before (though I just learnt there is indeed one in my city) and thought maybe Fannie and Freddie were the names of realtors in a movie or TV show! Went down a bit of a rabbit hole learning about GSEs as we don’t have a real equivalent here in Canada. Fascinating stuff (for this Canuck!)

One of the most amazing puzzles I've ever found. A Thursday from April 12, 2012 (!) by Michael Shteyman. One clue/answer combination: "Founder of the 26-/21- down, who died on April 12, 1912 (!)" CLARABARTON And then the 26 / 21 down was... AMERICAN REDCROSS And there was a set of crossing blocks in the middle of the puzzle, between those two answers, which were all... red. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=4/12/2012&g=39&d=D ....

Was "For the record, I think 58A would be an exceptional Final Jeopardy question" meant to be tongue-in-cheek? Because they have used a similar clue, Final Jeopardy Monday, December 13, 2010, in category COUNTRIES: "In only 2 cases can you add 2 letters to one country & get another country: Austria/Australia & this pair" though they later acknowledged there was one more that would have been accepted that didn't come up, Mali/Malawi. Obviously the one in this puzzle is the only one that fits the restriction of the final 2 letters, still fun!
DAP ?  I did fine on the rest but this one threw me.  Oh well, learn something every day.

Having grown up in a town right beside Manchester-by-the-Sea, I was delighted to see Cape Ann in this puzzle! Once simply Manchester, the town changed its name officially while I was in high school. My father, in between bouts of rolling his eyes, threatened to start a petition to rename our town "Hamilton-by-the-Dump" in recognition of the lovely landfill that sat right on the border of our illustrious communities.

And... the absolutely strangest puzzle I've ever encountered. A Sunday from April 1 (!), 1984 by Frances Hansen with the title: "!Pu Smottob" And that title was the clue for each of the theme answers. Some examples: LOOFLIRPAEHTSEMOCEREH YAWYMNOMIEMROFTIAWUOY ELURDROWSSORCAKAERBOT YADOTDIRGEHTPUGNIOGMI (and those were all across answers) Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=4/1/1984&g=47&d=A ....

The answer of the Pentatonic scale for the clue, “a common musical progression” , is kind of misleading. A better clue would be  “a common five note musical scale”. Or, “musical scale often found in old folk songs”.

When thinking of a “musical progression”, something having to do with chords would make more sense because musicians often refer to a “progression” as the series of chords, or harmony, that support the tune, or melody.
I appreciate all of the guest and new commentators, but I’m also missing fun and funny Sam Corbin’s take on things. Wherefore art thou, Sam?
ENNEADS / SERE / AER was a little much for me!
Definitely a little much for a Tuesday as much as seasoned vets on here will tell you otherwise. A little reliant on proper nouns, and some odd clueing. Not a great puzzle imo
Once again I muse how relatively recently these comments were FULL of vitriol about the puzzle being dumbed down. Now it’s too hard. 😢
I very much did not like the rectangles. They break up the words that contain them much more than the circles.

Stopping by again to share a recommendation:

In yesterday's NYT Gameplay newsletter, there is an article by Joel Fagliano called "Diary of a Puzzle Editor" that makes for a delightful read.

Alas, I found it much too short. 
Here's hoping it becomes a book!
Actually, I would say that the defining film of 1939 was "Gone With the Wind".

the clues being written in a way to make the answer 'pop' or make you laugh or smile or go AHA is what it's all about the less rote trivia the better this isn't Jeopardy its much deeper - fun puzzle keep hateful orgs mega etc out of puzzles anything that reeks of hate please refrain and easy words like Star Wars and 3 letter words nobody likes or uses have a nice day y'all

Good word play column and I thought the photo was dreamy. My best wishes for your time on Jeopardy, Nikita! 

I enjoyed this slightly different Tuesday puzzle. 
Two drink minimum? Well Ok and hoping Brad
gets a Saturday, too!
The villain in Silence of the Lambs was actually Buffalo Bill, no?
Can’t explain it, but man did I hate this puzzle. Will keep playing but holy smokes was that not for me.
Well, I must say - this puzzle humbled me.......
Seems like I may be in the minority, but I really enjoyed this one. Was pretty much right on my average time for a Tuesday. Cheers!
What an abomination for a Tuesday.
Thanks i hate it

@Paul

Congrats, Paul!  As always, I'm 121 behind you on the streak.

@Paul
Great streak!
Agree. In volleyball, they stand on the stand.
Whereas in tennis, they ump from their rump.

@Barry Ancona

Oh, c'mon! I thought this was actually pretty hard for Tuesday, and judging by the comments I was not the only one to get that impression.
Andrzej, Yes, it seems most people did find this on the hard side, despite the theme giving them an extra shot at solving. https://xwstats.com/puzzles/2026-05-19

@Barry Ancona

If the editors had taken out the boxes for the drinks, this could have been a decent Wednesday, where you don't see the theme until you solve the revealer, and you get a nice "A-ha!" moment.

@Barry Ancona

I'm a worse climber than you are a cruciverbalist, yet here goes: being moderately good at the sport, I have no idea whether an easy route is a 4+ or 5a (in the European system). To me they are both equally easy, and I just can't tell the difference. Probably that's the sort of thing that happened with you today?

Andrzej,
From later posts, I see there are a few other commenters who knew most of the "words" and "proper nouns" in this puzzle from the clues, for whom the addition of the theme made the solve even faster (and also fun). Even a few years ago, I think this would have been considered an easy Tuesday by most solvers. Today, clearly not.

@Barry Ancona

That's fair.

@Barry Ancona I did solve without overlays (as usual), but even after completing the puzzle I was stumped about the theme.

When I turned overlays on it became obvious.  My solve without overlays was a wee bit slower than my Tuesday average.
@Dave K. I've had that happen on the mobile app and resolve after logging in via a web browser on desktop.
@John Thanks, went to the website and was able to just type in any letter and it finished. Thankfully only about 12s were added to my actual time.

@Dave K.

If you're solving on the web, refreshing the page usually works to return the clock to the finish time.
@Selective Walrus was thinking the same thing. My music theory mind way overthinks any of the hints relating to music

@Selective Walrus

And then there's me, who's never heard of a PENTATONIC SCALE, and who has no idea what 2-5-1 refers to 🤣
@Selective Walrus   Agree!  This is an error.  A pentatonic scale is a scale, not a progression.  A progression is something like a I-IV-V or a II-V-I chord progression.

@Andrzej   ----  Pentatonic scale -- That is all the black keys on your piano.  You can play songs with just those alone.  They were used by early musicians before the other notes were discovered.  You can hear them in Hebrew prayer songs.

@sandi

@patk
So, I guess I’m the only one who first thinks of James Fenimore Cooper’s Leatherstocking Tales when I see the term Hawkeye. What one reads at a young age tends to remain with you.
@patk It’s funny, I thought of M*A*S*H, too, but I think it’s a reference to Iowan Hawkeyes (mascot for U of Iowa).
@MB Honestly, my first impulse there was “Pierce” but I gave up the notion pretty much right away.

@Sue S
He's a good man.

@Mark Smith
Indeed, the Leatherstocking Tales appear to be the origin of the nickname. In "The Prairie," an aging Natty Bumppo heads west, because the East is getting too crowded and civilized. Nebraskans felt the same way.

@patk am I the only one whose first thought was of the lamest Avenger?  Also, am I the only one who realized that this was a tongue-in-cheek comment?

I’m also a big fan of M*A*S*H, and of course both Benjamin Franklin Pierce and Clint Barton got their nicknames from the character in The Last of the Mohicans. And google tells me that the Iowan nickname (adopted in 1838, before Iowa was even a state) was to honor Chief Black Hawk, but also inspired by The Last of the Mohicans (published 12 years prior). So it all comes back to James Fenimore Cooper. 🤓

commentBody: @Al in Pittsburgh That's quite a roster of amazing films all in one year. I wonder what the state of the union was at that time that caused so many now-classic films to appear all at once. I'd say "The Wizard of Oz" is right up there as a defining film. It has been transmogrified through numerous iterations up until now! I believe it is still shown on TV every holiday season--unlike "Gone with the Wind" which you have to look around to find, I think. A bit off topic maybe but here's a fascinating modern film based on a true story: "Lion." https://www.imdb.com/title/tt3741834/

@Al  in Pittsburgh

And Victor Fleming directed both Gone With the Wind and Wizard of Oz which is a life achievement resume for any director right there. There is a wonderful play, “Moonlight and Magnolias” which makes the community theater rounds regularly about how David O Selznick pulled him off The Wizard of Oz towards the end to get in a room with him and scriptwriter Ben Hecht for two weeks to brainstorm how to save GWTW. Try to find it if you can; it was never adapted to a movie.

@Al  in Pittsburgh
it's a crazy good year when ten films are nominated for best film and The Women isn't one of them.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Ιασων

This aggression will not stand, man.
@Ιασων  I'm having an old friend for dinner.

@Mental State 
OK, so there's more than one slang term for the small room containing a toilet. On a boat/ship/submarine, it's usual to refer to that room as "the head", and (at least here in North America) some people will say things like "I'm just going to hit the head" instead of "I'm going to the toilet". So head = toilet. 

In the UK, where Hogwart's School of Witchcraft and Wizardy is sorta-kinda located - or at least where the station is that you catch the train that gets you there - a common term for that small room is "the LOO". 

So "The heads of Howard's?" is looking for the plural of the UK term for the small room with a toilet in it - or LOOS.

I hope that helps, because I don't think I can make it any clearer.

@Mental State the norm in this puzzle is that using any word that refers to a toilet and combining it with something quintessentially British will translate that word (eg john) into LOO or sometimes LAV

The cultural gulf across the pond is a chasm.

@Grumpy

Hogwarts is unambiguously located on the island of Great Britain. It's shielded from view by various enchantments, but it's not an alternate dimension like Narnia. It's a physical plot of land in Britain

@Andrzej, my brother was a DJ at a radio station years ago (this would have been in the 80s). I visited the studio and was confused for a bit by the signs for the toilets. They were labeled Elton, and Olivia Newton.

@Seth A

Is tonic alcoholic where you live?

@Seth A 

Or tea?

@Andrzej

Hair tonic may be alcoholic. But I wouldn’t drink it.

Also, I really wanted the themed clues to have two drinks in them. As of right now, I’m only seeing one in each. But I didn’t even understand the theme until I finished the whole puzzle and looked back. :)

Really though, that is because I was just moving along with all the clues — I didn’t find the puzzle too hard for Tuesday (as some have said in the comments).

Reminds me of a funny story. I was at a wedding. I don’t drink alcohol but I often order tonic (quinine) water with lime. The very young bartender was working under the supervision of an older bartender who was standing back. I said, "Tonic water and lime, please?" "You’re asking for tonic and water?" 

We went back and forth a few times and the older guy, trying so hard not to laugh I was afraid he’d burst a blood vessel, wasn’t about to rescue this poor kid who finally understood when I said, "Gin and tonic, hold the gin," at which point the older guy was laughing so hard he turned blue! Turns out the kid had only ever dispensed tonic water from the multi-mixer nozzle behind the bar by pressing the button marked T. If he’d seen a bottle of the stuff, he'd have seen "water" in the name. I gave him a big tip for having to go through that!

@Ed H.

Before 1989 Coca-Cola and Pepsi were the only foreign-licensed, Western-branded soft drinks in Poland. Everything else was made under Polish brands by Polish factories. This included tonic. It was called just that - tonic (or maybe the polonized "tonik" - it was over 35 years ago so I can't remember the details; "tonik" is the Polish word for "tonic water"). When Western brands appeared on our market after 1989, some of them used some English on their labels - which is why I was already in my teens when I first saw the term "tonic water". I was so surprised I remember it to this day.

@Ed H.

"Gin and tonic, hold the gin."
😂 

That should be in a movie script.
Classic line waiting to happen!

@Andrzej Correction: Tangentially related to alcohol, aka "gin & tonic"

No error. AMIE is feminine, and it takes the usually masculine MON because it begins with a vowel.

@Rob in French you use "mon" instead of "ma" when the word begins with a vowel, to help with pronunciation. So ma cherie but mon amie.

@Bruce

There's a bunch of regular commenters who would disagree with you.  I think that some of the less prolific commenters did not experience it, for some reason, so they thought it didn't exist.

But it did.  For a long time.

It's much like the missing Midi that many people have been complaining about.  I had literally never experienced it for weeks after it debuted, but once--and I do mean just once--it didn't show up.  I got the screen with just the two crossword choices, like it was before the Midi started, and I followed the advice I'd read for those who were complaining.  I refreshed.  And magically, the Midi did appear.  It has never happened again.

The Games are haunted, I tell you.  By emus, probably.

@Alex

This is probably more information than you want, but it may help with future interstate highway related clues.

Interstate highways in the US are named with odd-numbered routes going north and south, and even-numbered routes going east and west.  In addition, the major routes are usually divisible by 5.

The north-south routes are numbered from I-5 on the west coast to I-95 on the east coast, and east-west routes are numbered from I-10 across the south to I-90 across the north.  All of those primary routes have 2-digit numbers.

If a third digit is prefixed to the route number, it designates a spur into a city (odd digit) or a beltway around an urban area (even digit).

Knowing the numbering system can help you decipher the clue, even if you don't know exactly where the highway is.  "Sch. whose campus is just off Interstate 405" -- I-5 is in the west, and I-405 must be a beltway around a large urban area.  Los Angeles would be a good bet.  And since the answer has 4 letters, try UCLA.

@dlr
Funny, I was just reading something about Route 666, which runs north/south through the Four Corners region. They re-designated it as Route 491, but the locals still call it "The Devil's Highway."

@dlr

Even simpler - if it's cluing a 4-letter school, it's usually UCLA.

@Alex

Harry Potter the series is not exclusively British; but the clue references Hogwarts, which is.

@Alex
Constructors need answers with many vowels, hence the frequency of Oreo.  UCLA is half vowels, so we see it often.  It’s helpful to think about what useful crossword classic might be described by an obscure clue, in my experience; never been to Los Angeles myself, so I405 means nothing to me, but with the L from FLAG (semaphore clue) UCLA was +90% likely.

@Jeremy 
That is not how French works, mon ami.
@Jeremy Amie starts with a vowel, so ma becomes mon. MON AMIE is in fact the correct form.

@Jeremy
You would be correct, if « amie »didn’t start with a vowel. There needs to be a liaison between an article and a noun that starts with a vowel, which is why we say « mon amie » even if « amie » is feminine.

Even more confusingly, “beautiful” in French is « beau » (masculine) or « belle » (feminine). So a beautiful female friend would be « ma belle amie ». But a beautiful male friend cannot be « mon beau ami » because « beau » ends with a vowel. So we say « mon bel ami » ! 
And don’t get me started on what we do if there’s more than one friend !!
@Jeremy https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2ICFtXx546A

@Ian,

I love these impromptu foreign language lessons out here. Who needs a Rosetta Stone when we’ve got this? 😄

@Jeremy

I think it's easier to understand if you realize that we have the same thing in English. We don't say "a apple." That would sound clumsy. We say "an apple." Same exact thing.

Think about it this way too: we have two different ways to pronounce "the."
We say "the (thuh) book."--before a consonant
But we say "the (thee) apple."--before a vowel

Here's something else: "mes beaux amis." We would pronounce this like: "may bow zah mee"
The rules for French elision are complex.

To make it more complicated, one might also think that we pronounce "an apple" as "uh napple".

See fun. It pays to learn a foreign language when someone is young. Then these things just are learned by osmosis.

commentBody:
@lucky13
My favourite example of this kind of stuff is the link between napkin/napery/nappie and apron. You've probably already guessed:  "a napron" was mistakenly understood to be an + apron, the listener assuming that the N wasn't part of the root but, instead, just a liaison in front of a vowel.

@lucky13

"To make it more complicated, one might also think that we pronounce "an apple" as "uh napple"."

In fact this has happened (both directions) in English: an apron used to be a napron, similar etymology to napkin, and an adder used to be a nadder; in the other direction, a nickname used to be an ekename. Faulty separation is fun!

Also, pea and cherry come from people interpreting pease (/peez/) and cherise (/shereez/) as plural...

(I'm not a linguistics nerd at all, noooo)

@lucky13 this is literally why people don’t learn other languages. An apple, a chair, a phone don’t have gender. And a friend can be any gender, there’s no need to specify their gender, if they identify with any at all. Gendered languages are dying.
